Ingredient: egg

Key odorants: 3-methylindole, 2,5-dimethylpyrazine, 2-methylbutyraldehyde, styrene, methyl butyrate, 1-octen-3-ol

Flavor descriptors: chicken, characteristic odor similar to acetaldehyde, civet, wild, green bean, sweet, unpleasant and irritating odor, unpleasant odor of wild radish, cabbage-like, unpleasant odor in high concentration but pleasant in dilute solution., fecal

These aroma compounds define the characteristic flavor of egg.

Ingredient: bakery products

Key odorants: 2,3-dimethylpyrazine, 2,5-dimethylpyrazine, 2-ethylpyrazine, 2-ethyl-3-methylpyrazine, 2-methylpyrazine, 2,3,5-trimethylpyrazine

Flavor descriptors: peanut butter, roasted, walnut, peanut, green, sweat, powdery, meat, grass, hazelnut

These aroma compounds define the characteristic flavor of bakery products.

Ingredient: bread

Key odorants: coumarin, methyl benzoate, 3-hexanone, geraniol, 2-ethyl-4-hydroxy-5-methyl-3(2h)-furanone, methanethiol

Flavor descriptors: characteristic odor similar to acetaldehyde, civet, citral, strong odor, cherry, unpleasant and irritating odor, characteristic, pleasant, floral odor, popcorn, butter

These aroma compounds define the characteristic flavor of bread.

Ingredient: rye bread

Key odorants: 2-methylbutyl acetate, 3-methylthiopropanol, violet leaf aldehyde, ethyl lactate, 3-(methylthio)propionaldehyde, damascenone

Flavor descriptors: civet, pleasant aromatic vanilla odor, smoky, sweet, fat, soup, sour, bean, potato, malt

These aroma compounds define the characteristic flavor of rye bread.

These aroma compounds define the characteristic flavor of wheaten bread.

Ingredient: white bread

Flavor descriptors: herb, peculiar odor, somewhat resembling the odor of benzaldehyde, green, alcoholic, sweet, ... almond-like odor ..., lavender, chemical, bitter, fragrant

These aroma compounds define the characteristic flavor of white bread.

These aroma compounds define the characteristic flavor of wholewheat bread.

\strokec4 Dining Out Chef's Night Out\ \pard\pardeftab720\partightenfactor0 \fs24 \cf0 \strokec5 \ \pard\pardeftab720\sa160\partightenfactor0 \f0\b\fs36 \cf2 \strokec2 Page 9\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \f1\b0\fs28 \cf4 \strokec4 The New American Chef\cb1 \uc0\u8232 \cb3 What to Drink with What You Eat\ A chef's hands holding a metal tray filled with bright red caviar, with a small orange garnish being added; various kitchen tools and ingredients are visible on the counter.\ \pard\pardeftab720\partightenfactor0 \fs24 \cf0 \strokec5 \ \pard\pardeftab720\sa160\partightenfactor0 \f0\b\fs36 \cf2 \strokec2 Page 10\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \f1\b0\fs28 \cf4 \strokec4 A close-up photograph of several hanging kitchen utensils (spoons, spatulas) against a warm-toned background.\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \f2\i \cf4 At times our own light goes out and is rekindled by a spark from another person. \f1\i0 \cb1 \uc0\u8232 \f2\i \cb3 Each of us has cause to think with deep gratitude of those who have lighted the flame within us. \f1\i0 \ \'97 ALBERT SCHWEITZER\ \pard\pardeftab720\partightenfactor0 \fs24 \cf0 \strokec5 \ \pard\pardeftab720\sa160\partightenfactor0 \f0\b\fs36 \cf2 \strokec2 Page 11\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \f1\b0\fs28 \cf4 \strokec4 To Daniel Boulud, Patrick O\'92Connell, and Jean-Georges Vongerichten \'97 the leading lights of culinary creativity of their generation \'97 whose sparks always rekindle our flame\ Gastronomy is the rational study of all related to man as he is eating. Its purpose is to keep humankind alive with the best possible food. \'97 JEAN-ANTHELME BRILLAT-SAVARIN (1755\'961826)\ In what art or science could improvements be made that

Gastronomy is the rational study of all related to man as he is eating. Its purpose is to keep humankind alive with the best possible food. \'97 JEAN-ANTHELME BRILLAT-SAVARIN (1755\'961826)\ In what art or science could improvements be made that would more powerfully contribute to increase the comforts and enjoyments of mankind? \'97 SIR BENJAMIN THOMPSON, COUNT RUMFORD (IN A 1794 ESSAY ON THE ART OF COOKERY)\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa160\partightenfactor0 \f0\b\fs36 \cf2 \strokec2 PREFACE\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \f1\b0\fs28 \cf4 \strokec4 \'93When we no longer have good cooking in the world, we will have no literature, nor high and sharp intelligence, nor friendly gatherings, nor social harmony.\'94 \'97 MARIE-ANTOINE CAR\'caME, CHEF (1784\'961833)\ \'93Good cooking is an art, as well as a form of intense pleasure\'85.A recipe is only a theme, which an intelligent cook can play each time with a variation.\'94 \'97 MADAME JEHANE BENO\'ceT, CHEF (1904\'961987)\ \'93You have to love either what you are going to eat, or the person you are cooking for. Then you have to give yourself up to cooking. Cuisine is an act of love.\'94 \'97 ALAIN CHAPEL, CHEF (1937\'961990)\ The first quotation suggests why we do what we do, while the others suggest\ \pard\pardeftab720\partightenfactor0 \fs24 \cf0 \strokec5 \ \pard\pardeftab720\sa160\partightenfactor0 \f0\b\fs36 \cf2 \strokec2 Page 12\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \f1\b0\fs28 \cf4 \strokec4 how. We published our first book in 1995, and it is

The first quotation suggests why we do what we do, while the others suggest\ \pard\pardeftab720\partightenfactor0 \fs24 \cf0 \strokec5 \ \pard\pardeftab720\sa160\partightenfactor0 \f0\b\fs36 \cf2 \strokec2 Page 12\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \f1\b0\fs28 \cf4 \strokec4 how. We published our first book in 1995, and it is exciting as we approach the publication of\'a0 \f2\i The Flavor Bible \f1\i0 \'a0in 2008 to witness the realm of good cooking as it reaches a new \'93tipping point.\'94\ No longer content simply to replicate others\'92 recipes, today\'92s cooks \'97 professionals and amateurs alike \'97 increasingly seek to create their own dishes. In doing so, they celebrate the creative process of cooking as much as the finished product.\ Cooking at its most basic level is a creative act, one of transforming food through the application of heat and the incorporation of other ingredients. But there are different orders of creativity, and merely following a recipe is a creative act of the most basic order, like painting by numbers.\ When accomplished cooks grow restless, they start to analyze instructions before following them to see if they can improve upon the results, thus raising the act of cooking to a creative act of a higher order. As their experience grows, cooks are able to bring greater intuition and even inspiration to their cooking.\ Traditional cookbooks are aimed at first-order cooks. Every cook owes

raising the act of cooking to a creative act of a higher order. As their experience grows, cooks are able to bring greater intuition and even inspiration to their cooking.\ Traditional cookbooks are aimed at first-order cooks. Every cook owes a debt of gratitude to those who have brought progress to cuisine throughout history \'97 those who famously codified classic cuisines through the painstaking chronicling of recipes, from Auguste Escoffier in France to others around the globe. Appreciation is also due to those who have elevated and expanded the range of available ingredients and techniques, the essential building blocks of cooking.\ Over the years, cookbooks have come to dictate precise measurement of ingredients along with instructions for their preparation and assembly, which has done much to improve the general accessibility of recipes. However, they also have come to provide a false sense of security for which the unsuspecting cook pays a price. When a recipe is rigidly scripted and blindly followed, it negates the cook\'92s own creative instincts and good judgment \'97 not to mention much of the pleasure of truly \'93being\'94 in the moment.\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \f2\i \cf4 \'93Great cooks rarely bother to consult cookbooks.\'94 \'97 CHARLES SIMIC, U.S. POET LAUREATE\cb1 \ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \f1\i0 \cf4 \cb3 Those with the urge to innovate had long been on their own in the

truly \'93being\'94 in the moment.\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \f2\i \cf4 \'93Great cooks rarely bother to consult cookbooks.\'94 \'97 CHARLES SIMIC, U.S. POET LAUREATE\cb1 \ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \f1\i0 \cf4 \cb3 Those with the urge to innovate had long been on their own in the kitchen until many adopted our 1996 book,\'a0 \f2\i Culinary Artistry \f1\i0 , as their muse. That book sought to break the mold of contemporary prescriptive cookbooks and to restore the creative instinct to chefs. 2006 2008\ We believe cooking will continue to evolve, and not only as a means of \'93doing\'94 (i.e., putting dinner on the table, or \'93problem-solving\'94 by \'93following a recipe\'94). Over time, we believe more people \'97 including, perhaps, yourself \'97 will have discovered it as a way of \'93being\'94 in the world. We have learned enough over the past decade or two to question why cooking is done one way versus another. This thoughtful sensory engagement leads to a store of experiences that allow us to bring more intuition to the cooking process, synthesizing what we\'92ve done before into innovative approaches to creating a dish. Ultimately, cooking offers the opportunity to be immersed in one\'92s senses and in the moment like no other activity, uniting the inner and outer selves. At these times, cooking transcends drudgery and becomes a means of meditation and even healing.\ It is little surprise to us, then, that when U.S. Poet Laureate Charles Simic was asked by the\'a0 \f2\i New York Times Magazine \f1\i0 \'92s Deborah Solomon earlier this year, \'93What advice would you give to people who are looking to be happy?\'94 his response was \'93For starters, learn how to cook.\'94\ We hope this book makes you happy \'97 literally.\ \'97\'a0 \f0\b KAREN PAGE AND ANDREW DORNENBURG \f1\b0 \'a0New York City April 2008\ \pard\pardeftab720\partightenfactor0

GRANCHER,\'a0 \f2\i CINQUANTE ANS \'c0 TABLE \f1\i0 \'a0(1953)\'a0 \f0\b FLAVOR = TASTE + MOUTHFEEL + AROMA + \'93THE X FACTOR\'94 \f1\b0 \ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \f0\b \cf4 Taste \f1\b0 \'a0= What is perceived by the taste buds\'a0 \f0\b Mouthfeel \f1\b0 \'a0= What is perceived by the rest of the mouth\'a0 \f0\b Aroma \f1\b0 \'a0= What is perceived by the nose \'93 \f0\b The X Factor \f1\b0 \'94 = What is perceived by the other senses \'97 plus the heart, mind, and spirit Our taste buds can perceive only four basic tastes: sweet, salty, sour, and bitter. The essence of great cooking is to bring these four tastes into balanced harmony to create deliciousness. It\'92s that simple \'97 and that difficult. After all, flavor is a function not only of taste, but also of smell, touch, sight, and sound. Because we\'92re human beings, other nonphysical factors come into play, including our emotions, thoughts, and spirits.\ Learning to recognize as well as manipulate both the obvious and subtle components of flavor

taste, but also of smell, touch, sight, and sound. Because we\'92re human beings, other nonphysical factors come into play, including our emotions, thoughts, and spirits.\ Learning to recognize as well as manipulate both the obvious and subtle components of flavor will make you a much better cook. This book will be your companion in the kitchen whenever you wish to create deliciousness.\ Learning to cook like a great chef is within the realm of possibility. However, it is something that is rarely taught; it must be \'93caught.\'94\ Everyone who cooks \'97 or even merely seasons their food at the table before eating \'97 can benefit from mastering the basic principles of making food taste great. This complex subject is simplified by one thing: while the universe may contain a vast number of ingredients and a virtually infinite number of ingredient combinations, the palate can register only the four basic tastes.\ Great food balances these tastes beautifully. A great cook knows how to taste, to discern what is needed, and to make adjustments. Once you learn how to season and how to balance tastes, a whole new world opens up to you in cooking. Of course, several factors conspire against your ever doing so \'97 not the least of which is a culture that sees the publication of thousands of new

to balance tastes, a whole new world opens up to you in cooking. Of course, several factors conspire against your ever doing so \'97 not the least of which is a culture that sees the publication of thousands of new cookbooks annually featuring recipes that promise to dazzle you and your guests if you follow them to the letter. And yet you\'92re often left wondering why the results aren\'92t as delicious as promised. That\'92s because great cooking is never as simple as merely following a recipe. The best cooking requires a discerning palate to know when a dish needs a little something or other \'97 and what to add or do to elevate its flavor.\ \f0\b WHAT IS PERCEIVED BY THE MOUTH \f1\b0 \ \pard\pardeftab720\partightenfactor0 \fs24 \cf0 \strokec5 \ \pard\pardeftab720\sa160\partightenfactor0 \f0\b\fs36 \cf2 \strokec2 Page 18\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \fs40 \cf2 Taste Buds\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \f1\b0\fs28 \cf4 \strokec4 Sweetness. Saltiness. Sourness. Bitterness. Every delicious bite you've ever tasted has been a result of these four tastes coming together on your taste buds. We taste them as individual notes, and in concert. Each taste affects the other. For example, bitterness suppresses sweetness. In addition, different tastes affect us in different ways. Saltiness stimulates the appetite, while sweetness satiates it. Take the time to explore the four basic tastes.\ \pard\pardeftab720\partightenfactor0 \fs24 \cf0 \strokec5 \ \pard\pardeftab720\sa160\partightenfactor0 \f0\b\fs36

the other. For example, bitterness suppresses sweetness. In addition, different tastes affect us in different ways. Saltiness stimulates the appetite, while sweetness satiates it. Take the time to explore the four basic tastes.\ \pard\pardeftab720\partightenfactor0 \fs24 \cf0 \strokec5 \ \pard\pardeftab720\sa160\partightenfactor0 \f0\b\fs36 \cf2 \strokec2 Page 19\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \fs28 \cf4 \strokec4 Sweetness \f1\b0 \ It takes the greatest quantity of a substance that is sweet (versus salty, sour, or bitter) to register on our taste buds. However, we can appreciate the balance and \'93roundness\'94 that even otherwise imperceptible sweetness adds to savory dishes. Sweetness can work with bitterness, sourness \'97 even saltiness. Sweetness can also bring out the flavors of other ingredients, from fruits to mint.\ \pard\pardeftab720\partightenfactor0 \fs24 \cf0 \strokec5 \ \pard\pardeftab720\sa160\partightenfactor0 \f0\b\fs36 \cf2 \strokec2 Page 20\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \fs28 \cf4 \strokec4 Saltiness \f1\b0 \ When we banished more than thirty of America\'92s leading chefs to their own desert islands with only ten ingredients to cook with for the rest of their lives ( \f2\i Culinary Artistry \f1\i0 , 1996), the number-one ingredient they chose was salt. Salt is nature\'92s flavor enhancer. It is the single most important taste for making savory food delicious. (Sweetness, by the way, plays the same role in desserts.)\ \f0\b Sourness \f1\b0 \ Sourness is second only to salt in savory food and sugar in sweet food in its

the single most important taste for making savory food delicious. (Sweetness, by the way, plays the same role in desserts.)\ \f0\b Sourness \f1\b0 \ Sourness is second only to salt in savory food and sugar in sweet food in its importance as a flavor enhancer. Sour notes \'97 whether a squeeze of lemon or a drizzle of vinegar \'97 add sparkle and brightness to a dish. Balancing a dish\'92s acidity with its other tastes is critical to the dish\'92s ultimate success.\ \pard\pardeftab720\partightenfactor0 \fs24 \cf0 \strokec5 \ \pard\pardeftab720\sa160\partightenfactor0 \f0\b\fs36 \cf2 \strokec2 Page 21\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \fs28 \cf4 \strokec4 Bitterness \f1\b0 \ Humans are most sensitive to bitterness, and our survival wiring allows us to recognize it in even relatively tiny amounts. Bitterness balances sweetness, and can also play a vital role in cutting richness in a dish. While bitterness is more important to certain people than to others, some chefs see it as an indispensable \'93cleansing\'94 taste \'97 one that makes you want to take the next bite, and the next.\ \f0\b Umami (Savoriness) \f1\b0 \'a0In addition to the four basic tastes, there is growing evidence of a fifth taste,\'a0 \f2\i umami \f1\i0 , which we first wrote about in 1996 in\'a0 \f2\i Culinary Artistry \f1\i0 . there is growing evidence of a fifth taste,\'a0 \f2\i umami \f1\i0 , which we first wrote about in 1996 in\'a0 \f2\i Culinary Artistry \f1\i0 . It is often described as the savory or meaty \'93mouth-filling\'94 taste that is noticeable in such ingredients as anchovies, blue cheese, mushrooms, and green tea, and in such flavorings as monosodium glutamate (MSG), which is the primary component of branded seasonings such as Ac\'b4cent.\ \pard\pardeftab720\partightenfactor0 \fs24 \cf0 \strokec5 \ \pard\pardeftab720\sa160\partightenfactor0 \f0\b\fs36 \cf2 \strokec2 Page 22\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \fs28 \cf4 \strokec4 Mouthfeel \f1\b0 \ In addition to its sense of taste, the mouth has a sense of \'93touch\'94 and can register other sensations, such as temperature and texture, that all play a role in flavor. These aspects of food, generally characterized as mouthfeel, help to bring food into alignment with our bodies, and bring some of a dish\'92s greatest interest and pleasure. The crunchiness and crispiness of a dish contribute sound as well as textural appeal.\ \pard\pardeftab720\partightenfactor0 \fs24 \cf0 \strokec5 \ \pard\pardeftab720\sa160\partightenfactor0 \f0\b\fs36 \cf2 \strokec2 Page 23\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \fs40 \cf2 Temperature\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \f2\i\b0\fs28 \cf4 \strokec4 I always pay attention to temperature. pay attention to temperature. I look at what I feel like eating now. If it is cold and rainy outside, I make sure that soup is on the menu. If it is hot outside, I make sure there are lots of salads on the menu. \f1\i0 \ \'97 ANDREW CARMELLINI, A VOCE (NEW YORK CITY)\ Temperature is one of the foremost among the other sensations that can be perceived by the mouth. Boston pastry chef Rick Katz, with whom Andrew cooked at Lydia Shire\'92s restaurant Biba, first taught him the lesson of pulling out the ice cream a few minutes before serving so that the slight rise in temperature could maximize its flavor.\ A food\'92s temperature can affect both the perception and enjoyment of a dish. our environment.\ A jar labeled "Walnuts in Lemon Honey" sits beside a block of cheese and slices of cheese topped with walnuts and honey.\ \pard\pardeftab720\partightenfactor0 \fs24 \cf0 \strokec5 \ \pard\pardeftab720\sa160\partightenfactor0 \f0\b\fs36 \cf2 \strokec2 Page 24\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \fs40 \cf2 Texture\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \f2\i\b0\fs28 \cf4 \strokec4 I would never serve pike on a base of chowder, because balance and texture are so important when it comes to creating a dish. Is there a rich component, a lean component, a crunchy component, and a cleansing component? Are all the taste sensors activated so that you want to go back for a second bite? Cod works better over a richer preparation like chowder. I would also make sure to choose the right technique for the cod: I would not poach it, because if it is poached it would be silky on silky. If it is seared, it is crunchy on silky \'97 which is more appealing because of the contrast. \f1\i0 \ \'97 SHARON HAGE, YORK STREET (DALLAS)\ A food\'92s texture is central to its ability to captivate and to please. We value pureed and/or creamy foods (such as soups and mashed potatoes) as \'93comfort\'94 foods, and crunchiness and crispiness (such as nachos and caramel corn) as \'93fun\'94 foods. creamy foods (such as soups and mashed potatoes) as \'93comfort\'94 foods, and crunchiness and crispiness (such as nachos and caramel corn) as \'93fun\'94 foods. We enjoy texture as it activates our other senses, including touch, sight, and sound.\ While babies by necessity eat pureed foods, most adults enjoy a variety of textures, particularly crispiness and crunchiness, which break up the smoothness of texture \'97 or even the simple monotony \'97 of dishes.\ \pard\pardeftab720\partightenfactor0 \fs24 \cf0 \strokec5 \ \pard\pardeftab720\sa160\partightenfactor0 \f0\b\fs36 \cf2 \strokec2 Page 25\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \fs28 \cf4 \strokec4 Piquancy \f1\b0 \ Our mouths can also sense what we often incorrectly refer to as \'93hotness,\'94 meaning piquancy\'92s \'93sharpness\'94 and/or \'93spiciness\'94 \'97 whether boldly as in chile peppers, or more subtly as in a sprinkle of cayenne pepper. a drying sensation caused by the tannins in red wine or strong tea, and occasionally in foods such as walnuts, cranberries, and unripe persimmons.\ \f0\b WHAT IS PERCEIVED BY THE NOSE \f1\b0 \ \pard\pardeftab720\partightenfactor0 \fs24 \cf0 \strokec5 \ \pard\pardeftab720\sa160\partightenfactor0 \f0\b\fs36 \cf2 \strokec2 Page 27\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \fs40 \cf2 Aroma\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \f1\b0\fs28 \cf4 \strokec4 Aroma is thought to be responsible for as much as 80 percent or more of flavor. This helps to explain the popularity of aromatic ingredients, from fresh herbs and spices to grated lemon zest. Incorporating aromatic ingredients can enhance the aroma of your dish and, in turn, its flavor.\ Some qualities are perceived through both the sense of taste and smell, such as:\'a0 \f0\b Pungency \f1\b0 \ Pungency refers to the taste and aroma of such ingredients as horseradish and mustard that are as irritating \'97 albeit often pleasantly \'97 to the nose as they are to the palate.\ \pard\pardeftab720\partightenfactor0 \fs24 \cf0 \strokec5 \ \pard\pardeftab720\sa160\partightenfactor0 \f0\b\fs36 \cf2 \strokec2 Page 28\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \fs40 \cf2 Chemesthesis\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \f1\b0\fs28 \cf4 \strokec4 Chemesthesis refers to other sensations that tickle (e.g., the tingle of carbonated beverages) or play tricks on (e.g., the false perception of \'93heat\'94 from chile peppers, or \'93cold\'94 from peppermint) our gustatory senses.\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \f0\b\fs40 \cf2 \strokec2 Heightening Flavor with Dominique and Cindy Duby of Wild Sweets\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \f2\i\b0\fs28 \cf4

of carbonated beverages) or play tricks on (e.g., the false perception of \'93heat\'94 from chile peppers, or \'93cold\'94 from peppermint) our gustatory senses.\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \f0\b\fs40 \cf2 \strokec2 Heightening Flavor with Dominique and Cindy Duby of Wild Sweets\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \f2\i\b0\fs28 \cf4 \strokec4 We believe that food preparation is 60 percent ingredients and 40 percent technique. \f1\i0 \ \'97 DOMINIQUE AND CINDY DUBY, WILD SWEETS (VANCOUVER)\ Flavor is the combination of the taste you experience on your tongue and the aroma you experience through your nose. We believe that as much as 90 percent of what we perceive as taste is actually aroma. When you eat a pineapple, the flavor really comes through the nose. So, if your pineapple is not ripe, it won\'92t have much aroma. It may taste sweet, but it won\'92t taste like pineapple.\ There are two ways to bring flavor to a dish, through aroma or through chemical reaction. We always say that cooking is no different from doing a lab experiment: The minute you add heat to a raw product, you are changing the status of that product. When you use the Maillard reaction \'97 which is what happens when you sear a piece of meat \'97 you are getting a reaction of caramelization from the carbohydrates and amino acids. This chemical reaction creates flavor.\ To add

product. When you use the Maillard reaction \'97 which is what happens when you sear a piece of meat \'97 you are getting a reaction of caramelization from the carbohydrates and amino acids. This chemical reaction creates flavor.\ To add aroma to a dish, think of a piece of fish cooked in broth with herbs or lemon. The problem is that the flavor escapes into the air. If you walk into a room and it smells great, that means there is not much flavor left in the dish. The aroma has escaped. So, if you want to add aroma to a dish, the best way is through sous-vide cooking [which cooks encased food at long, slow temperatures]. This method traps the aroma into what you are cooking without letting it escape.\ The problem is that sous vide is not available for home cooks. What a home cook can do is \'93sealed cooking,\'94 where you take a heavy-duty freezer zip lock bag, put in what you want to cook with the liquid, then cook it over a steady heat on your stove. Another method that works is putting the bag in a pot with a single-cup water heater that goes to about 140 degrees Fahrenheit and, from time to time, stirring the water. [Note: Care must be taken with this low-temperature

stove. Another method that works is putting the bag in a pot with a single-cup water heater that goes to about 140 degrees Fahrenheit and, from time to time, stirring the water. [Note: Care must be taken with this low-temperature method of cooking to avoid food poisoning.]\ This is a way to put \'97 and keep \'97 a lot of flavor in whatever you are\ \pard\pardeftab720\partightenfactor0 \fs24 \cf0 \strokec5 \ \pard\pardeftab720\sa160\partightenfactor0 \f0\b\fs36 \cf2 \strokec2 Page 29\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \f1\b0\fs28 \cf4 \strokec4 cooking.\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \f0\b \cf4 WHAT IS PERCEIVED BY THE HEART, MIND, AND SPIRIT \f1\b0 \ \f0\b \'93The X Factor\'94 \f1\b0 \ When we are present to what we are eating, food has the power to affect our entire selves. We experience food not only through our five physical senses \'97 including our sense of sight, which we address first below \'97 but also emotionally, mentally, and even spiritually.\ \f0\b Compatible Flavors \f1\b0 \ An essential aspect of great cooking is harnessing compatible flavors \'97 which involves knowing which herbs, spices, and other flavorings best accentuate particular ingredients.\ A process of trial and error over centuries resulted in classic cuisines and dishes, some of which feature timeless combinations of beloved flavor pairings \'97 for example, basil with tomatoes, rosemary with lamb, and tarragon with lobster.\ However, today it\'92s possible to use

trial and error over centuries resulted in classic cuisines and dishes, some of which feature timeless combinations of beloved flavor pairings \'97 for example, basil with tomatoes, rosemary with lamb, and tarragon with lobster.\ However, today it\'92s possible to use scientific techniques to analyze similar molecular structures to come up with new, compatible pairing possibilities, as odd as some might sound \'97 such as jasmine with pork liver, parsley with banana, or white chocolate with caviar.\ A close-up of a red, gelatinous food item, possibly a mousse or jelly, with a yellow center, on a light-colored surface.\ \pard\pardeftab720\partightenfactor0 \fs24 \cf0 \strokec5 \ \pard\pardeftab720\sa160\partightenfactor0 \f0\b\fs36 \cf2 \strokec2 Page 30\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \fs40 \cf2 The Visual\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \f1\b0\fs28 \cf4 \strokec4 The visual presentation of a dish can greatly enhance the pleasure we derive from it. Just a few decades ago, it was still possible to taste a dish with the eyes, but only those who\'92d spent time in world-class kitchens knew the tricks of such artistic plate presentation. Since the advent of\'a0 \f2\i Art Culinaire \f1\i0 \'a0and the Web, it\'92s become easier to reproduce a great dish\'92s elaborate form than its exquisite flavor.\ How a dish looks can also affect our perception of its flavor in more direct ways; for example, the deeper the color of a berry sorbet, the more berry flavor

reproduce a great dish\'92s elaborate form than its exquisite flavor.\ How a dish looks can also affect our perception of its flavor in more direct ways; for example, the deeper the color of a berry sorbet, the more berry flavor is perceived. The stronger the connection between a particular food and a particular color, the stronger the flavor impact \'97 such as berries with red, lemon with yellow, and lime with green.\ \pard\pardeftab720\partightenfactor0 \fs24 \cf0 \strokec5 \ \pard\pardeftab720\sa160\partightenfactor0 \f0\b\fs36 \cf2 \strokec2 Page 31\ \pard\pardeftab720\partightenfactor0 \f1\b0\fs24 \cf6 \strokec6 The Emotional\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \f2\i\fs28 \cf4 \strokec4 I say all the time that [my mother\'92s Spanish potato and egg tortilla] is my favorite because it conveys a point: that sentimental value comes above all else. \f1\i0 \cb1 \uc0\u8232 \cb3 \'97FERRAN ADRIA, EL BULLI (SPAIN)\ We taste with our hearts as much as with our tongues. What else could explain adult preferences for one\'92s mother\'92s dishes over those prepared by a great chef? This also helps to explain the lasting appeal of traditional dishes and cuisines of countries around the globe, which stem from our love for their cultures, their people, and the deeply rooted culinary traditions that have sustained them over centuries.\ A close-up photograph of a thin, translucent sheet of food topped with a dark, crispy piece, resting on a white surface.\ \pard\pardeftab720\partightenfactor0

love for their cultures, their people, and the deeply rooted culinary traditions that have sustained them over centuries.\ A close-up photograph of a thin, translucent sheet of food topped with a dark, crispy piece, resting on a white surface.\ \pard\pardeftab720\partightenfactor0 \fs24 \cf0 \strokec5 \ \pard\pardeftab720\sa160\partightenfactor0 \f0\b\fs36 \cf2 \strokec2 Page 32\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \fs40 \cf2 The Mental\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \f1\b0\fs28 \cf4 \strokec4 If we ate only for sustenance, we could probably survive on nutritive pills and water. But we also eat for pleasure. Because we typically eat three times a day, 365 days a year, we enjoy novelty, such as a twist on the traditional construct of a dish. Increasingly, since the 1980s and the advent of \'93tall\'94 food, chefs have played with the presentation of their ingredients. Since the 1990s, the advent of avant-garde cuisine and so-called molecular gastronomy has seen chefs experiment more and more with both the chemical composition and presentation of dishes as well.\ \pard\pardeftab720\partightenfactor0 \fs24 \cf0 \strokec5 \ \pard\pardeftab720\sa160\partightenfactor0 \f0\b\fs36 \cf2 \strokec2 Page 33\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \fs40 \cf2 The Spiritual\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \f1\b0\fs28 \cf4 \strokec4 The preparation, cooking, and eating of food is a sacrament. Treating it as such has the potential to elevate the quality of our daily lives like nothing else. Several of the world\'92s leading chefs have worked to perfect each aspect of the dining encounter \'97

of food is a sacrament. Treating it as such has the potential to elevate the quality of our daily lives like nothing else. Several of the world\'92s leading chefs have worked to perfect each aspect of the dining encounter \'97 from the food and drink to the ambiance to the service \'97 to raise the overall experience to a new level imbued not only with pleasure, comfort, and interest, but also with meaning.\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \f0\b\fs40 \cf2 \strokec2 Choosing a Cooking Technique with Michael Anthony of Gramercy Tavern in New York City\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \f1\b0\fs28 \cf4 \strokec4 When we look at an ingredient, we ask, \'93How can we maximize the inherent flavor or quality of what this is?\'94 As in any other progressive modern kitchen, there is a fascination with examining all the new techniques we can get our hands on. We have used sous vide [i.e., cooking vacuum-packed ingredients at low temperatures for long periods of time], but we are far from letting any technique drive a dish.\ Alice Waters described something cooked via sous vide as \'93dead\'94 food. I can understand her opinion because she is all about inflecting that \'93fresh-cut crunch\'94 feeling into her food. Sous vide is all about a long, slow cooking process \'97 and those products calling for that [such as tougher cuts of meat] will

understand her opinion because she is all about inflecting that \'93fresh-cut crunch\'94 feeling into her food. Sous vide is all about a long, slow cooking process \'97 and those products calling for that [such as tougher cuts of meat] will be awesome.\ Why we choose any specialized piece of equipment for a dish always gets back to good old-fashioned cooking principles: What is the best way of capturing flavors?\ That is how choosing a technique fits into my cooking.\ I\'92m excited to eat in restaurants that are pushing the boundaries of presentation and technique. Yet my personal take on food that is too technically driven is that technique comes first and taste comes second. I feel the meals that hit home are ones where the flavor is there and you are eating a meal in a distinct time and place. I love it when people look back on a meal, and the time of year is what made it special. The ingredients they tasted seemed naturally a part of that moment because that is what is available then.\ Sometimes straightforward flavors are the ones people can latch on to, even though the ingredients can be very sophisticated behind the scenes. If, ultimately, the flavor combination is one that is simple and straightforward, with an impressive balance of acidity and bitterness,

flavors are the ones people can latch on to, even though the ingredients can be very sophisticated behind the scenes. Sometimes the meals that hit home are not the ones that were the most complicated.\ \pard\pardeftab720\partightenfactor0 \fs24 \cf0 \strokec5 \ \pard\pardeftab720\sa160\partightenfactor0 \f0\b\fs36 \cf2 \strokec2 Page 34\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \fs40 \cf2 FLAVOR FROM THE INSIDE OUT\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \f1\b0\fs28 \cf4 \strokec4 America's foremost chefs reached the pinnacle of their profession through their painstaking attention to every aspect of their cuisine and the restaurant experience. only of their cuisines but of the creation and orchestration of ambiance and service as well, chefs such as\'a0 \f0\b Daniel Boulud \f1\b0 \'a0of New York's Restaurant Daniel and\'a0 \f0\b Patrick O'Connell \f1\b0 \'a0of The Inn at Little Washington in Virginia transcend the prior three categories to bring the dining experience to another level in the\'a0 \f0\b spiritual \f1\b0 \'a0realm.\ In the pages that follow, we'll share chefs' reflections on working in the first three realms. and ask what I did to it. Often the answer is \'93Very little.\'94\ Our zucchini salad is a perfect example of celebrating what comes to our door. We get baby zucchini and we shave them raw. Then we add a flavored pecan oil, raw local pecan halves, shaved pecorino cheese, Mexican marigold, and a pinch of salt.\ We also think a lot about the best way to present these ingredients. \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \f0\b \cf4 Dan Barber of Blue Hill at Stone Barns in Pocantico Hills, New York \f1\b0 \'a0Our pork dish starts not at the table with the cut of pork on the plate, but in the field with what kind of pork we choose to raise for our restaurant. We keep the dish pretty straightforward. We will serve it with Brussels sprout leaves and chickpeas.\ We don\'92t want to do anything to hide the flavor. when creating dishes \'97 from the bounty of a particular country and its historic evolution into a national cuisine, to the classic dishes of that culture, and of the families and even individual cooks within it \'97 as each might bring a unique twist to the standards.\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa160\partightenfactor0 \f0\b\fs36 \cf2 \strokec2 Maricel Presilla of Cucharamama and Zafra in Hoboken, New Jersey\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \f1\b0\fs28 \cf4 \strokec4 I\'92m Cuban \'97 but whether you are Cuban, Venezuelan, Chilean [or another nationality], there is an enormous pride in the flavors of your region and of your childhood. It is like mother\'92s milk, your first compass. It doesn\'92t matter how much you travel or how much you expand your palate: You always come back to this notion of basic flavors and ingredients, time and again.\ For me, being a Cuban from eastern Cuba is my anchor. I am from Santiago, which has a very defined cuisine. It is more influenced by the neighboring islands such as Jamaica and Haiti, which both have European influences as well. The cooks from those islands brought flavors with them like allspice, which is not used in the rest of Cuba. My family uses allspice lavishly in our adobo and in other dishes. I use it a lot as well \'97 and it is one of my favorite spices. Our

like allspice, which is not used in the rest of Cuba. My family uses allspice lavishly in our adobo and in other dishes. I use it a lot as well \'97 and it is one of my favorite spices. I have traveled throughout South America, cooking with chefs as well as elderly women, not to mention studying the history of our cuisine from pre-Columbian cooking through the influence of Spanish medieval cooking.\ What is fascinating is that there is a lot of structure to our cooking and clearly defined rules to our seasoning.\ I have learned how all these flavors work in their nationalities and regions, making Latin America my \'93backyard\'94 of flavor. \'a0While working in India for eleven months, I visited Gujarat. Having been raised in the United States, to me \'93Indian food\'94 was what my mom cooked. I had no idea there were other kinds!\ If you watch a Bollywood movie, you see that the Punjabis and the Gujaratis make a lot of fun of each other. The Gujaratis find us Punjabis very volatile and show-offy. They will say, \'93Just like your cuisine, you are all fiery and hot!\'94 We Punjabis, on the other hand, think the Gujaratis are quiet and dull. Of course, for me, that has all changed \'97 I love all Indians.\ In Gujarat, at the end of cooking a curry, they would add a teaspoon of sugar and a half a lime. The first time I tasted this, it was awful \'97 but then I found out that the cook of the house was a crappy cook! When I had it done correctly, it was delicious, and had a nice, mellow aftertaste. So, I incorporated lime into my cooking after eating Gujarati food in India.\ My kitchen is staffed by Punjabi women, who had a hard time using lime because it was not part of their cooking in Punjab. Neither is Charlie Trotter\'92s [also in Chicago] or Daniel [in New York City]. Someone could say that these chefs are just cooking for themselves, and that is true to a certain extent. I am doing this style of [avant-garde] cooking because I am bored with other dishes. If I was all about the customer and just wanted to make people happy, I would cook paella, pizza, and burgers all day. Everyone would have smiles on their faces!\ There is a little bit of selfishness, but there is also playfulness. We have to make sure guests are happy, and that our dishes are both inventive and seasonal. What is happening [in the world] right now is that we are expanding our repertoire of what good food is.\ A dessert plate featuring a chocolate-covered sphere, a strawberry, a cube of

that our dishes are both inventive and seasonal. Then it evolves into something where we tweak the flavor a little bit to make it taste more like it \'93should.\'94 For example, once we wanted to make a cookie with a really concentrated flavor. So, we threw cookies into the dehydrator, and turned them into powder. This created a new building block for flavor. [Instead of the flour you would normally use in your dough,] you weigh the powdered cookies out as your starch in your normal cookie recipe. But this starch is now a carrier of flavor for the end product \'97 so the resulting cookie now tastes more like it \'93should\'94

your dough,] you weigh the powdered cookies out as your starch in your normal cookie recipe. But this starch is now a carrier of flavor for the end product \'97 so the resulting cookie now tastes more like it \'93should\'94 than it would have just using regular flour.\ A lot of our food is rooted in classic combinations \'97 and it has to be. Why? The process by which we go about creating is so foreign that we have to make something that you are familiar with eating. In a tapas restaurant, you have had generations of trial and error to create a dish through local ingredients and techniques that have evolved into something that tastes good. For example, olives marinated with garlic and parsley has been around for a hundred years. Now, there is nothing wrong with that. But, for us, the idea of marinating olives with garlic and parsley is not fun. So, we are going to make a dish where the olives are actually the parsley and the garlic is the olives and so on. tastes like something you\'92d want.\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \f0\b \cf4 What\'92s in a Pancake? \f1\b0 \'a0Yes, we do serve real food. We got some BLiS syrup [handcrafted aged maple syrup] in the kitchen which is used by other top chefs around the country. This stuff is liquid gold! This syrup is aged in small bourbon barrels in Canada and is $20 for a small [375 ml] bottle. The maker even hand-stamps each bottle with wax and writes the label by hand. When we got it in, we knew we had to do something with it.\ We decided to make a pancake dish. We started with the question \'93How can we make this dish taste more like pancakes than pancakes?\'94 So, we pureed cooked pancakes, then adjusted the liquid with milk. The cool thing about making a pancake puree out of cooked pancakes is that you can alter the concentration levels. You can\'92t alter the concentration levels when you are making straight pancakes.\ We wanted to trick the customer into thinking that they were going to eat a hot pancake topped with this syrup. Believability is so important in the process, so we even execute the dish in front of them [in the dining room]. We bring out a metal plate that looks hot [but is frozen and steaming from being immersed in

syrup. Believability is so important in the process, so we even execute the dish in front of them [in the dining room]. We bring out a metal plate that looks hot [but is frozen and steaming from being immersed in nitrogen]. The pancake batter is then shot from a syringe. The batter freezes when it hits the metal. When we serve it, we top it with the BLiS syrup. You have people in a foreign country stepping all over these grapes, putting foot fungus in there, and then creating a fermented thing that is controlled. I don\'92t think that wine is all that natural! Every time you turn on the blender and puree something, you are crossing the line of natural versus unnatural. Nature did not intend for an electric motor to spin a blade and turn a solid into a liquid.\ If

Nature did not intend for an electric motor to spin a blade and turn a solid into a liquid.\ If you think an unripe green tomato tastes better than a ripe heirloom red tomato picked off the vine in August, then by all means eat the green tomato. That is flavor preference. What if I feed you an unripe green tomato but alter its taste so it tastes\'a0 \f2\i better \f1\i0 \'a0than a vine-ripened red tomato? When we pick it off the vine earlier, it has a different quality \'97 it is sharper. So, we will pair something that maybe should not go with it, like Parmesan cheese pureed with butter. This will make it a little richer and compensate for the overtannic, overacidic qualities of the tomato. [If you enjoy it,] then you have succumbed to not following the seasons.\ In the same manner, we\'92ll serve a dish of unripe things that taste ripe because they are together! What do I prefer? Of course I prefer the vine-ripened tomato at the end of August. do I prefer? Of course I prefer the vine-ripened tomato at the end of August. We would make the other dish just to ask the question.\ \f0\b Creating New Flavors \f1\b0 \'a0I disagree with the notion that there are no new flavors. Maybe there are no new products, even though we don\'92t know everything that exists in the sea. To create a new flavor, all you have to do is dissect an ingredient. For example, if I take an avocado and put it into a centrifuge, we would separate the fat and the water. The water will carry the avocado taste. If I take that water and create a snow with it or make it into a pill, it will have a much different taste than avocado, because avocado is thought of as something that is rich. But there would be no richness here; it is a completely different product. We just made up a new flavor.\ So now if I want to mimic an avocado, I would have to serve something rich with it. I could serve something dairy with it, like brown butter. I now have something that would go with a classical turbot dish with brown butter and capers, whereas fresh avocado would not have paired up with that dish very well.\ \f0\b How to Lengthen Flavors

I will think, \'93How long do I want a flavor to last? Do I want a blast, or do I want it to linger?\'94\ We make a dish of a fried

\'93a long finish\'94 that you taste long after swallowing]. I will think, \'93How long do I want a flavor to last? Do I want a blast, or do I want it to linger?\'94\ We make a dish of a fried oyster with shiso and a wasabi, yuzu, and sancho pepper [also known as Szechuan pepper] sauce. That is a great way to experience length of flavor. The shiso wraps around the oyster like a taco shell. When you take a bite, you get the herbaceous punch from the shiso, then richness from the fried oyster that coats your tongue. Then you get the acid from the yuzu on the sides of your tongue. When you swallow the bite, the wasabi comes up the back of your nose. Then you get a small surprise because the pepper gives you a numbing effect across your palate. This is not a \'93two seconds of heaven\'94 bite; we are talking about a twenty-second experience. We have orchestrated this not in a crazy chemical way, but just by playing with an oyster.\ The cues come from the wine world: Sometimes you want bright and spritzy high-acid flavor, and other times you want something rich and long. \'97 BRAD FARMERIE, Public (New York City)\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \f0\b \cf4 Katsuya Fukushima of minibar in Washington, DC \f1\b0 \

from the wine world: Sometimes you want bright and spritzy high-acid flavor, and other times you want something rich and long. \'97 BRAD FARMERIE, Public (New York City)\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \f0\b \cf4 Katsuya Fukushima of minibar in Washington, DC \f1\b0 \ I like to work with classic, preexisting flavor combinations that people can recognize. They may not recognize anything from the textures I use, but they will find recognition in the flavors.\ To make our \'93Philly Cheesesteak,\'94 we start with the bread. We put pita dough through a pasta machine so that it gets really thin, but puffs way up when you cook it. For the cheese element, we use a Vermont and Wisconsin cheddar cheese mousse that we pipe into the pita. For the beef, we serve seared Kobe beef. For the onions, we spread on caramelized onion puree. We then top that with truffles. So you have bread, cheese, beef, and onion, just like a Philly cheesesteak. We add the truffles just to push it over the top.\ \f0\b CHEFS\'92 STRATEGIES FOR BALANCING FLAVORS \f1\b0 \ *On every forkful \'97 regardless of what\'92s on the fork \'97 there has to be salt, acid,\ \pard\pardeftab720\partightenfactor0 \fs24 \cf0 \strokec5 \ \pard\pardeftab720\sa160\partightenfactor0 \f0\b\fs36 \cf2 \strokec2 Page 45\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \f1\b0\fs28 \cf4 \strokec4 and heat. . . . However, unless you are serving a pepper-crusted dish, or a lemon or vinegar dish, your seasoning should never be detected. Instead, your beans should taste like beans, and your rabbit should taste like rabbit. Diners don\'92t need to know how much salt, acid, and heat are in the dish \'97 and none should be obvious. You\'92ll also have minor supporting players in a dish, such as the aromatic or picante levels that might come from your mirepoix or sofrito [a well-cooked sauce of tomatoes, garlic, onions, and herbs] or whatever else went in there. But when a diner tastes your dishes, all you want them to be thinking about is those beans, or that rabbit. \'97 SHARON HAGE, YORK STREET (DALLAS)\ Over the past fifteen years, we have interviewed many chefs in an effort to understand how some of America\'92s best chefs approach creating great food. What we learned is that there are as many approaches as chefs themselves. It is the key to making food taste good.\ The same is true in pastry. I am constantly working with my pastry chef when I taste desserts with her. I will taste something and say it is too sweet \'97 that it is missing the acid balance, and needs some fat and a little salt. Using salt in pastry brings out the flavor, just as it does in savory food. When it comes to dessert, people think more in a \'93monochromatic\'94 sweet fashion. But sweets need balance, too.\ My favorite flavor is the harmony of these elements working together, whether it is sweet or savory.\ \f0\b Marcel Desaulniers of The Trellis in Williamsburg, Virginia \f1\b0 \'a0My cooking philosophy has always been simple: Don\'92t complicate things, and let the food speak for itself. I want the food to leave a clean taste in your mouth, and that goes for savory food as well as desserts.\ We don\'92t use

philosophy has always been simple: Don\'92t complicate things, and let the food speak for itself. I want the food to leave a clean taste in your mouth, and that goes for savory food as well as desserts.\ We don\'92t use a lot of spices because they can have an intensity of flavor that can overwhelm other flavors. People can\'92t restrain themselves with certain ingredients \'97 and, often, it\'92s garlic. At The Trellis, we use garlic in only one recipe: a dressing that has been on the menu for twenty-six years. Herbs can be overused as well, too. Rosemary and basil, which I love, are constantly overused, which results in a bitter flavor.\ I tell cooks, \'93When in doubt, don\'92t use it.\'94 The food you are starting with\ \pard\pardeftab720\partightenfactor0 \fs24 \cf0 \strokec5 \ \pard\pardeftab720\sa160\partightenfactor0 \f0\b\fs36 \cf2 \strokec2 Page 46\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \f1\b0\fs28 \cf4 \strokec4 already has its own intrinsic flavor, whether it is a scallop or a filet of beef. Whatever you do to embellish the flavor should be a very minor part of the relationship, rather than sharing the stage fifty-fifty.\ I thought of myself as a saucier from my days in New York, yet when I opened this restaurant in Williamsburg, it was not the way I wanted to present the food. I thought of myself as a saucier from my days in New York, yet when I opened this restaurant in Williamsburg, it was not the way I wanted to present the food. Rather than sauces, I wanted to use vegetables and fruits as the accompaniments. They provide moisture as a natural component and they work on the plate, but not as a fifty-fifty partner.\ An example of where fruit works great as a component is on our pork dish: We serve pork loin medallions, grilled sausage, sweet potatoes, tender cooked string beans, and bourbon-glazed peaches. So this is a dish with no sauce, except the juice from the peaches.\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \f0\b \cf4 Carrie Nahabedian of Naha in Chicago \f1\b0 \ You should always season something right from the start, and not just at the end. If you just add salt and pepper at the end, you are not doing a soup justice. You want those flavors to blossom.\ Take our butternut squash soup as an example. We start our soup with hefty pieces of slab bacon, so we are already starting off with a lot of flavor. From there, we add the mirepoix and make sure it gets nice and caramelized. Then we start adding our salt, fresh-cracked pepper, and thyme sprig.\ Next, we add roasted squash. The reason we

starting off with a lot of flavor. From there, we add the mirepoix and make sure it gets nice and caramelized. Then we start adding our salt, fresh-cracked pepper, and thyme sprig.\ Next, we add roasted squash. The reason we roast it before it goes in the soup is because you can taste what state the squash is in. It may need a little help because it needed to age a couple more weeks in the cellar before we got it, so we may add a garnet yam. Now, we taste the soup and decide what kind of sweetness to add. That could be honey, molasses, or maple sugar \'97 not just [white] sugar.\ Next, we puree the soup and pass it [through a fine-mesh strainer] to achieve its smooth texture.\ To the soup base, we add the garnishes. We already have bacon, but some smoked duck will be good, too. We add spaghetti squash and fried parsnips for additional texture and, in the case of the parsnips, sweetness as well. For a last note, we add a drizzle of maple syrup or barrel-aged sherry. These things just seal the soup so that the flavors all escalate. It is important to not rush things so that your flavors come together.\ \pard\pardeftab720\partightenfactor0 \fs24 \cf0 \strokec5 \ \pard\pardeftab720\sa160\partightenfactor0 \f0\b\fs36 \cf2 \strokec2 Page

of fish sauce to cut the richness.\ When you are adjusting the seasoning of a dish, you need to think about its origin. That is why traveling is so important. \pard\pardeftab720\partightenfactor0 \fs24 \cf0 \strokec5 \ \pard\pardeftab720\sa160\partightenfactor0 \f0\b\fs36 \cf2 \strokec2 Page 48\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \f1\b0\fs28 \cf4 \strokec4 second, I add the fat component \'97 the oil or butter \'97 to the sauce, which brings it all together.\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \f0\b \cf4 Brad Farmerie of Public in New York City \f1\b0 \'a0When I come up with a new dish, I am looking at two things: The first is building up the richness, and the second is cutting that richness with acidity, spice, or herbaceousness.\ I start by thinking, What is the rich component? How can I build richness? If it is a protein, we may cure the meat or fry the fish. If it is a vegetarian dish, I may start with eggplant, which is meaty, so I will build it up with miso or tahini, which will make it even richer and meatier. This is really important in a vegetarian dish, because I hate those flimsy veg plates of grilled vegetables and a lot of salad!\ Texture is another way of building richness. If you add miso or tahini, that adds richness and texture. An emulsification also gives a sense of richness in your mind and across your palate. One way to add rich texture in a \'93fakey-jakey\'94 way is to add palm sugar. It is [a less] sweet sugar, and we will

texture. An emulsification also gives a sense of richness in your mind and across your palate. One way to add rich texture in a \'93fakey-jakey\'94 way is to add palm sugar. It is [a less] sweet sugar, and we will shave it into a dish halfway through the cooking and temper it with tamarind. We use it so subtly that you don\'92t even know it is there.\ Now that I have my richness, the question becomes, \'93How can we cut that richness so it is light on the palate and in the belly?\'94 We use a tremendous amount of acid, but it is always in check with what is on the plate. Choosing the right acid or herb will leave a light feeling on the palate and cut a rich dish.\ With any fried food, you need a big zap of acidity to get through it. With fried fish, we will use preserved lemon, yogurt, or yuzu with a white soy dipping sauce.\ Cured meat is almost the epitome of richness. Cured meats have a salinity to them, and quite often there is sugar to balance the salt as well. You need some punchy flavors to cut the meat, or it will just be heavy on the palate. The family of apples, pears, and quince also holds up very well, as do sour poached plums.\ I like aromatic spices [for their ability to cut richness]. If I\'92m working on a dish inspired by India, my inspirations are the flavors of clove, cardamom, and coriander seed. They are aromatic spices that really cut the fat of a dish, so the dish is not big, fat, and flabby on the palate. Fresh turmeric gives you fruitiness and upfront flavor with a touch of acid that perks up a dish. If you add fresh turmeric to your curry, you will make a world of difference by adding this one small thing.\ If I\'92m working on a dish inspired by Southeast Asia, I like galangal, lemongrass, and ginger. If you eat a piece of fresh fruit by itself, it is a dessert. So you want the dessert, in the end, to taste better than the fruit itself. To do that, you need to add things that go naturally. Blueberries and peaches go well together. Vanilla goes with practically any fruit. Almonds go with most fruits because they are light nuts that have a lot of flavor but not a lot of fat. It comes from the heart, makes great demands on the palate, and needs enthusiasm and a deep\ \pard\pardeftab720\partightenfactor0 \fs24 \cf0 \strokec5 \ \pard\pardeftab720\sa160\partightenfactor0 \f0\b\fs36 \cf2 \strokec2 Page 52\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \f1\b0\fs28 \cf4 \strokec4 love of food to bring it to life.\ \'97 GEORGES BLANC, MICHELIN THREE-STAR CHEF IN VONNAS, FRANCE\ The defining trait of a great cook is more than a great palate, and more than great technique; it is sound judgment. It is not only knowing what to do with ingredients, but also when, where, why, and how to serve them. Cooking involves the thoughtful combination and manipulation of ingredients. Good cooking results in those ingredients tasting even better. Great cooking not only celebrates the ingredients, but also celebrates the moment.\ Recipes evolved as a way to teach less experienced cooks how to prepare particular dishes, by providing specific proportions of compatible ingredients along with step-by-step instructions. However, anyone who believes that every recipe followed verbatim will always produce consistent results is kidding themselves, given the diversity within individual ingredients, whether the sweetness of fruit or the thickness of a fish fillet. Slavish followers of recipes, who treat

anyone who believes that every recipe followed verbatim will always produce consistent results is kidding themselves, given the diversity within individual ingredients, whether the sweetness of fruit or the thickness of a fish fillet. Slavish followers of recipes, who treat them as gospel instead of guidelines, make the mistake of putting more faith in someone else\'92s instructions than they do in themselves. Many people would do better in the kitchen if they didn\'92t blindly follow recipes. In fact, following recipes may be holding you back from achieving your potential as a cook.\ Take the time to learn and master some of the general principles of how to make food taste great \'97 such as what to pair with various ingredients, and how to prepare them \'97 which is the subject of this book. After all, more than a dozen years after the advent of the Food Network turned every TV set in this country into a virtual 24/7 cooking school, supplementing the burgeoning food programming on many of the other major channels, Americans are better-trained cooks than at any previous point in history. Most of us can now whip up a dish with our general knowledge of how to boil pasta, saut\'e9 an onion, or grill a steak. Today, long, detailed recipes are rarely needed for most cooking (with the

point in history. Most of us can now whip up a dish with our general knowledge of how to boil pasta, saut\'e9 an onion, or grill a steak. Today, long, detailed recipes are rarely needed for most cooking (with the exception of baking, whose chemistry requires precise measurements).\ Training wheels can be useful when first learning to ride a bike, as can \'93painting by numbers\'94 when first learning to paint. Likewise, following a recipe can be useful when first learning to cook \'97 for understanding the order of completing certain steps, and internalizing their intrinsic logic. But great cooking should be more akin to meditation: you \'97 and all of your senses \'97 are in the moment, and fully awake and aware. You can taste the ingredients, and know what you need to do to make them taste their best. what the weather is and decide what the soup wants to be.\'94 Be fully grounded in the moment that is part of that second, that minute, that hour, that day, that month, that season, that year of your life \'97 all at once.\ To elevate your cooking to a whole new level, develop a better appreciation for the essence of ingredients, which provides insight into when and how to best use them. Celebrating the essence of ingredients allows you to work with them more intuitively and effectively. This book will help you decide what ingredients to reach for in the kitchen, and why, and what to do with them when you do.\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa160\partightenfactor0 \f0\b\fs36 \cf2 \strokec2 HOW TO MAKE FOOD TASTE GREAT\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \f1\b0\fs28 \cf4 \strokec4 A great cook is able to make food taste great by doing two basic things:\ \pard\tx220\tx720\pardeftab720\li720\fi-720\sa80\partightenfactor0 \ls1\ilvl0\cf4 \kerning1\expnd0\expndtw0 \outl0\strokewidth0 {\listtext 1 }\expnd0\expndtw0\kerning0 \outl0\strokewidth0 \strokec4 Understanding the essence of the\'a0 \f0\b moment \f1\b0 , which comprises everything from the meal\'92s driving force to the occasion, to the weather, to the available time, budget, and/or other resources (for example, ingredients, equipment, etc.).\cb1 \ \ls1\ilvl0\cb3 \kerning1\expnd0\expndtw0 \outl0\strokewidth0 {\listtext 2 }\expnd0\expndtw0\kerning0 \outl0\strokewidth0 \strokec4 Understanding the essence of the\'a0 \f0\b ingredients \f1\b0 , which comprises their season, regionality, weight and volume, function, flavor, and/or flavor affinities.\cb1 \ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \cf4

resources (for example, ingredients, equipment, etc.).\cb1 \ \ls1\ilvl0\cb3 \kerning1\expnd0\expndtw0 \outl0\strokewidth0 {\listtext 2 }\expnd0\expndtw0\kerning0 \outl0\strokewidth0 \strokec4 Understanding the essence of the\'a0 \f0\b ingredients \f1\b0 , which comprises their season, regionality, weight and volume, function, flavor, and/or flavor affinities.\cb1 \ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \cf4 \cb3 The deeper your understanding of both, the greater your ability to bring them together into a dish that is the perfect expression of the ingredients and the moment.\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa160\partightenfactor0 \f0\b\fs32 \cf2 \strokec2 Understanding the Essence of the Moment\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \f1\b0\fs28 \cf4 \strokec4 Why do you need or want to cook in the first place? In a day and age when more of the meals we consume are prepared\'a0 \f2\i outside \f1\i0 \'a0the home than\'a0 \f2\i at \f1\i0 \'a0home, cooking has become relatively infrequent. So, what\'92s driving you?\ Always keep in mind your starting point in the kitchen, the reference point serving as the inspiration for your cooking \'97 which can be virtually anything. Following its essence logically or intuitively will lead you to each subsequent step toward creating something delicious. Your starting point is your initial desire. It may be an ingredient, or a dish, or a country: you\'92re excited to prepare the first heirloom tomatoes of summer; you\'92re craving your grandmother\'92s cheesecake; or you want a dish that will take you to Italy to relive the flavors you

how to proceed.\ \pard\pardeftab720\partightenfactor0 \fs24 \cf0 \strokec5 \ \pard\pardeftab720\sa160\partightenfactor0 \f0\b\fs36 \cf2 \strokec2 Page 55\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \fs40 \cf2 Occasion\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \f1\b0\fs28 \cf4 \strokec4 Even if it wasn\'92t the driving force behind why you\'92re cooking in the first place, you\'92ll still want to consider the importance and significance of the meal. A quick dinner on a time-pressed weeknight will be different from a dinner made on the weekend when there is more time to enjoy the sensual process of cooking. Keep the occasion in mind when planning your meal, with the knowledge that any meal can be elevated to a special occasion \'97 or simplified in a crunch. The elements of breakfast take on a different level of importance when they are used for a special brunch. A quick fried egg and a toasted English muffin on a Tuesday can morph into poached eggs with Canadian bacon with hollandaise sauce on an English muffin for a weekend brunch, or vice versa.\ But a true special occasion \'97 a birthday, anniversary, or holiday \'97 definitely calls for something equally special to celebrate it. If lack of time is a factor, a birthday isn\'92t the time to undertake making and decorating a cake from scratch. From left to right, the visible labels read \'93SUMMER SAVORY,\'94 \'93tarragon,\'94 \'93orange mint,\'94 and \'93SAGE.\'94 Green leafy plants grow at the base of the stakes.\ \pard\pardeftab720\partightenfactor0 \fs24 \cf0 \strokec5 \ \pard\pardeftab720\sa160\partightenfactor0 \f0\b\fs36 \cf2 \strokec2 Page 56\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \fs40 \cf2 Weather\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \f1\b0\fs28 \cf4 \strokec4 While seasonality will drive your choice of ingredients, the weather will drive your decision as to how to prepare and serve them. On the coldest days, you\'92ll want to warm your home as well as your body with slow-braised dishes, soups, and stews, and on warmer days, you\'92ll want to keep your home (and yourself) cooler by doing more grilling or on the hottest days serving quick-cooked or even chilled dishes \'97 no matter what season those days may fall in.\ Some cooks believe you can serve anything at any time of the year. by doing more grilling or on the hottest days serving quick-cooked or even chilled dishes \'97 no matter what season those days may fall in.\ Some cooks believe you can serve anything at any time of the year. Our eyebrows have raised upon seeing heavy braised dishes on restaurant menus in August. However, chefs have defended them by arguing that once someone is seated in an air-conditioned dining room, they forget what it\'92s like outside! Those chefs may be giving customers what they want while they are at the table . . . but what happens when those same customers leave the restaurant after finishing their braised short ribs and walk out into the heat and humidity? They might feel as out of sorts as they would wearing long wool overcoats on such a night! A lighter meal on a hot night won\'92t sap your energy in the same way. Even in the middle of summer, there will be unseasonably cool days that might suggest a warm dish that would offend the sensibilities on the season\'92s hottest, most sweltering day. Even during the dead of winter, the sun can break through and create an unseasonably warm day on which hot, slow-cooked braised dishes would not be appealing.\ Considering the weather means taking into consideration the natural rhythms of nature. Even during the dead of winter, the sun can break through and create an unseasonably warm day on which hot, slow-cooked braised dishes would not be appealing.\ Considering the weather means taking into consideration the natural rhythms of nature. One of the easiest clues available to inform your cooking is to look out the window, and ask yourself what you feel like eating. If you look out the window on a sunny summer morning, are you more tempted by hot oatmeal \'97 or by granola and yogurt? That afternoon, as the temperature rises, does your mouth water thinking of hot tomato soup \'97 or chilled gazpacho? That night, does the temperature fall enough to make you want to turn on the oven to roast a chicken \'97 or would you rather grill it on your stovetop?\ On our first visit to a certain New York City restaurant on one of the hottest nights of summer, we were surprised to be sent an\'a0 \f2\i amuse-bouche \f1\i0 \'a0from the kitchen that was a tall glass of hot yellow pepper soup. While the soup itself might have been made from seasonal ingredients and tasty in a vacuum, unfortunately what we remember even more vividly was how unappealing we found it, especially as our first taste after a sweltering taxi ride to the restaurant.

If the same soup had been served to us cold, we expect it would have been more successful as the welcoming gesture it was intended to be.\ \pard\pardeftab720\partightenfactor0 \fs24 \cf0 \strokec5 \ \pard\pardeftab720\sa160\partightenfactor0 \f0\b\fs36 \cf2 \strokec2 Page 57\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \f1\b0\fs28 \cf4 \strokec4 Understanding the Essence of the Ingredients Once you\'92re clear about \'93the problem\'94 of what to cook, the right ingredients, well prepared, provide the ideal \'93solution.\'94 To prepare any ingredients well, it\'92s vital to understand and respect their essence.\ What do we mean when we talk about an ingredient\'92s \'93essence\'94? Every ingredient has associations with it, and the sum total of those associations comprises its essence. An ingredient\'92s essence is more than its flavor. Take two sample ingredients, both salty:\ \pard\tx220\tx720\pardeftab720\li720\fi-720\sa80\partightenfactor0 \ls3\ilvl0\cf4 \kerning1\expnd0\expndtw0 \outl0\strokewidth0 {\listtext \uc0\u8226 }\expnd0\expndtw0\kerning0 \outl0\strokewidth0 \strokec4 What comes to mind when you think of soy sauce? Common associations besides its saltiness might include Asia (region), rice (as a common flavoring for), and/or scallions (compatible ingredient).\cb1 \ \ls3\ilvl0\cb3 \kerning1\expnd0\expndtw0 \outl0\strokewidth0 {\listtext \uc0\u8226 }\expnd0\expndtw0\kerning0 \outl0\strokewidth0 \strokec4 What comes to mind when you think of Parmesan cheese? Aside from saltiness (flavor), its common associations might include Italy (region), pasta or pizza (which it is a common flavoring for), and/or basil and tomatoes (compatible ingredients).\cb1 \ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \cf4 \cb3 While certain other ingredients \'97 such as chicken, garlic, and onions, which are all used around the world \'97 may be neutral in their universality, many\ A basket of bread and a loaf of bread on a table.\ \pard\pardeftab720\partightenfactor0 \fs24 \cf0 \strokec5 \ \pard\pardeftab720\sa160\partightenfactor0 \f0\b\fs36 \cf2 \strokec2 Page 58\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \f1\b0\fs28 \cf4 \strokec4 ingredients are rooted in distinct associations.\ Some of the primary aspects of any ingredient\'92s essence include its seasonality, its\'a0 \f0\b taste \f1\b0 , its\'a0 \f0\b volume \f1\b0 , its\'a0 \f0\b function \f1\b0 , its\'a0 \f0\b regionality \f1\b0 , its\'a0 \f0\b weight \f1\b0 , and its\'a0 \f0\b flavor affinities \f1\b0 . While the importance of each factor varies, depending on the dish, the goal when cooking is to ensure that all of these factors are respected in the ingredient\'92s use.\ \pard\pardeftab720\partightenfactor0 \fs24 \cf0 \strokec5 \ \pard\pardeftab720\sa160\partightenfactor0 \f0\b\fs36 \cf2 \strokec2 Page 59\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \fs40 \cf2 Seasonality\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \f2\i\b0\fs28 \cf4 \strokec4

on the dish, the goal when cooking is to ensure that all of these factors are respected in the ingredient\'92s use.\ \pard\pardeftab720\partightenfactor0 \fs24 \cf0 \strokec5 \ \pard\pardeftab720\sa160\partightenfactor0 \f0\b\fs36 \cf2 \strokec2 Page 59\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \fs40 \cf2 Seasonality\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \f2\i\b0\fs28 \cf4 \strokec4 It is almost a clich\'e9 to talk about seasonality if you are a New American chef. You are cooking that way naturally. I had a customer complain that his favorite pea ravioli was not on the menu. I explained that peas were not very good at the time, so that we were featuring corn ravioli. I don\'92t focus on making an ingredient that is not at its peak taste better; I simply take it off the menu. Chefs still pay more attention to seasonality than customers do. We still have diners who want berries in the middle of February! \f1\i0 \ \'97 ANDREW CARMELLINI, A VOCE (NEW YORK CITY)\ Cooking with ingredients at their seasonal peak is such a central tenet of good cooking that it bears constant repeating. In stores across the country, you can find almost any ingredient at almost any time of year. However, an ingredient\'92s mere availability offers no assurances of its quality.\ Each season suggests a different palette of ingredients, and different ways of preparing and serving them. Classic holiday dishes represent time-tested ways of

at almost any time of year. However, an ingredient\'92s mere availability offers no assurances of its quality.\ Each season suggests a different palette of ingredients, and different ways of preparing and serving them. Classic holiday dishes represent time-tested ways of celebrating the season: Picture a grilled hamburger followed by a red, white, and blue strawberry shortcake accented with blueberries and whipped cream served on the Fourth of July, or roasted turkey with stuffing, cranberry sauce, and pumpkin pie on Thanksgiving. (If you\'92re not convinced of the perfection of each dish\'92s expression of the season and occasion, just imagine eating each menu on the other holiday!) Each season also suggests its own beverages; for example, summer calls more often for lighter-bodied white and ros\'e9 wines, just as winter calls for fuller-bodied reds.\ \pard\pardeftab720\partightenfactor0 \fs24 \cf0 \strokec5 \ \pard\pardeftab720\sa160\partightenfactor0 \f0\b\fs36 \cf2 \strokec2 Page 60\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \fs40 \cf2 Taste\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \f1\b0\fs28 \cf4 \strokec4 Every ingredient has its\'a0 \f2\i stereotypical \f1\i0 \'a0taste (bananas are sweet), plus its\'a0 \f2\i actual \f1\i0 \'a0taste, which may be a function of its age or ripeness. For example, a banana may increase noticeably in sweetness as it ripens and its color changes from green to yellow to brown. That is why it\'92s crucial to taste your ingredients when cooking. If you don\'92t \'97 and, as a result, don\'92t end

banana may increase noticeably in sweetness as it ripens and its color changes from green to yellow to brown. That is why it\'92s crucial to taste your ingredients when cooking. If you don\'92t \'97 and, as a result, don\'92t end up making other adjustments (for example, slightly decreasing the amount of sugar used when using very ripe bananas) \'97 you\'92ll find your dishes to be out of balance. Even seemingly similar ingredients (such as regular versus aged balsamic vinegar, or Italian versus Thai basil) can vary dramatically.\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa160\partightenfactor0 \f0\b\fs36 \cf2 \strokec2 York Street\'92s Sharon Hage on Classic Flavor Pairings\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \f1\b0\fs28 \cf4 \strokec4 I try to eat a lot of different foods and read a lot of different types of books to try to break away from classic pairings. I have had a million pork dishes, and I keep thinking that there has to be something else besides pork with sage! I try all these different things then turn to another cook and say, \'93Got any sage?\'94\ There is a reason for tomatoes with basil, and for lamb with mint. What I don\'92t make is lamb with mint jelly \'97 but there will likely be some mint someplace on the dish.\ Roasted beets work with salty cheese, whether it is [Italian] ricotta salata, or [Mexican] queso fresco, or another country\'92s

What I don\'92t make is lamb with mint jelly \'97 but there will likely be some mint someplace on the dish.\ Roasted beets work with salty cheese, whether it is [Italian] ricotta salata, or [Mexican] queso fresco, or another country\'92s version of salty cheese.\ The fun part is to discover what the classic pairing is, then present it to people in a way that makes them think it is different, while still being true to you as a chef. It would be hard to imagine summer tomatoes without the basil component. We might add some chives or mint or sumac, but the dish will still have some basil as well.\ \pard\pardeftab720\partightenfactor0 \fs24 \cf0 \strokec5 \ \pard\pardeftab720\sa160\partightenfactor0 \f0\b\fs36 \cf2 \strokec2 Page 61\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \fs40 \cf2 Weight\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \f1\b0\fs28 \cf4 \strokec4 It was through our study of wine that we developed an appreciation for the critical role of understanding a wine\'92s body, or weight \'97 and, in turn, the relative weights of various foods. In fact, weight has eclipsed color as the key factor in pairing wine with food.\ Weight and season often go hand in hand, as we crave lightness in summer and heavier dishes when temperatures fall. In summer, that craving for lightness could be satisfied with a salad of fresh greens topped with shrimp or chicken and tossed in

often go hand in hand, as we crave lightness in summer and heavier dishes when temperatures fall. In summer, that craving for lightness could be satisfied with a salad of fresh greens topped with shrimp or chicken and tossed in a vinaigrette. Our winter cravings for more substance and warmth might lead us to a hearty stew made with red meat and root vegetables in their own thick sauce.\ There is a spectrum of wine and ingredients that \cf2 \strokec2 Page 62\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \fs40 \cf2 Volume\ \pard\pardeftab720\sa240\partightenfactor0 \f1\b0\fs28 \cf4 \strokec4 One important aspect of an ingredient\'92s flavor essence is its \'93volume.\'94 Think of a stereo dial with \'931\'94 indicating a \'93quiet\'94 seasoning of chopped parsley, and \'9310\'94 suggesting a \'93loud\'94 mound of freshly chopped habanero chile peppers. You\'92ll use them very differently to create very different effects, while striving to achieve the same all-important balance in the final dish.\ So, is the ingredient you\'92re working with quiet, moderate, or loud? You need to be aware of an ingredient\'92s volume whenever combining it with other ingredients. If a dish is overspiced to the point where you can\'92t taste its essence,\ A plated dish with meat, peas, and a sauce, with a fork and knife on the plate.\ it\'92s wrong.
